text: An Economic Theory of Salvation Religioneijia Li † Shaoda Wang ‡ April 11, 2025 [A preliminary draft.

Please do not circulate or cite without author permission.] Abstract This paper constructs a unified model that micro-founds the three types from Max Weber’s typology of world religions, “mystic religions,” “activist religions,” and “religions of world adjustment.” We then employ the model to study the interaction between world religions and secular institutions.
Among others, the model offers a novel, religion-based explanation for the great “reversal of fortune” between the East and the West.

Precisely because China and India were the economic powerhouses in the Middle Ages, they failed to sustain religious activism that facilitated modern economic growth and constitutional government in Western Europe.

The model further interprets competition over socioeconomic status as a partial substitution of salvation religions, explaining why status competition is fierce where the influence of salvation religions have declined.
∗ We benefit especially from the many conversations with Sascha Becker, Sebastiano Della Lena, Gerard Roland, and Yang Xie.

We also thank insightful comments from Robert Akerlof, Sam Bazzi, Jeanet Sinding Bentzen, Arthur Campbell, Jean-Paul Carvalho, Quoc-Anh Do, Ruixue Jia, Erik Kimbrough, Kaveh Majlesi, Jared Rubin, Ferenc Szucs, Ludger Woessmann, and participants of ASREC 2024 Conference at Chapman University.
All errors are our own. † Monash University.
Email: weijia.li@monash.edu. ‡ University of Chicago and NBER. Email: shaoda@uchicago.edu.
Why did modern capitalism emerge in the West, not in China or India?

This “Great Divergence”(Pomeranz; Greif ) has been attributed to different cultural orientations that have deep religious roots (Weber; Tillich; McCleary and Barro; Alesina and Giuliano; Mokyr; Becker et al. ).
In China, Confucianism affirms and “adjusts” to the secular world, therefore unable to revolutionize the traditional economy.
The secular world is devalued by Indian religions and Judeo-Christianity.

But while Indian religions ultimately encourage a minimization of worldly actions to attain a “mystical escape” from the corrupt world, many denominations of Judeo-Christianity command their followers to maximize their worldly actions as an “active” instrument of God, potentially capable of revolutionizing the secular world.
This typology of world religions is summarized by Figure 1.
World religions Religions that are adjusted to the world (e.g.
Confucianism) Religions in tension with the world; Religions of salvation Mystic religions that renounce the world (e.g.
Buddhism) Activist religions that master the world (e.g.

Calvinism) Figure 1: Weber’s typology of world religions (adapted from Swedberg and Agevall ) In this paper, we construct a model to jointly micro-found the three types in Max Weber’s typology: “religions of world adjustment,” “mystic religions,” and “activist religions.” Foundational to this typology is a psychological definition of “salvation,” the starting point of our analysis.
Salvation here refers to a “permanent situation” of “an internal immunity against suffering” (pp.218, Weber; Riesebrodt; Swedberg and Agevall ).

This definition focuses on the psychological immunity that results from faith in religious salvation, directing our attention to how the quest for the psychological immunity interacts with the economy and political order (Schluchter; Whimster ).
Our baseline model formalizes this psychological immunity as follows.
For a follower who acts as demanded by the religion, he should not psychologically suffer even when he is inflicted with disasters.

But if a follower does not act as demanded, the religion warns that the follower will suffer from disasters. 1 One can immediately see that salvation religions must impose commandments on human action that are extremely onerous.
Under a commandment too difficult to comply, a follower never fully obeys the commandment, failing to attain the action that could have protected the follower from suffering.
Such a follower can always make sense of suffering: he suffers because he disobeyed his God, who has imposed a just punishment on his disobedience.
Here emerges a remarkable pattern.
Every time the follower suffers, the traumatic experience strengthens his faith in his religion.
These religions are not only stable against suffering.
They even transform suffering into an occasion to vitalize faith.
By contrast, if a religion imposes commandments that are too “moderate,” the follower will eventually act as demanded by the religion.
But when the follower suffers again, he cannot make sense of his suffering: he has done exactly what his religion demands, yet he still suffers.
The experience of suffering eventually falsifies the promise of “world-adjusting religions,” which affirms action according to one’s own nature.
The analysis explains why there are two opposite types of salvation religions, mysticism that demands a minimal worldly action and activism that demands a maximal worldly action.
An individual of no religious concerns balances between the secular benefit and cost from worldly action, thus preferring an intermediate level of action.
Because of this secular preference for intermediate action, a salvation religion can only sanctify either minimal or maximal action.
For followers of mystic religions, full escape from worldly action would endanger their material or social existence.
These followers move towards but never completely attains full escape, allowing them to attribute suffering to their lingering entanglement with the corrupt world.
Analogously, followers of activist religions intensify their worldly action but never fully obey the commandment of overzealous action.
Central to our analysis, we then build on the baseline model to examine the interactions between salvation religions and secular institutions.

First, we explain why Judeo-Christianity tended to be far more activist than salvation religions of China and India (Weber; Tillich ), uncovering an economic foundation for the “religious divergence” between the East and the West.
In our model, activist religions are unlikely to emerge where the material reward for worldly action is already high.

The high material reward forces salvation religions to command the opposite direction of mystic escape, now an especially superhu This “commandment for salvation” has been central to social scientific analysis of salvation religions (pp.218, Weber ).

Notice that even under Predestination in Calvinism, the psychological “certainty of grace” is still confirmed by human action, a famous insight from Weber and Troeltsch: a Calvinist can confirm that he is actually among the “elected” by his active striving in the world.
man commandment because individuals must renounce the lucrative material reward from productive action.

In the Middle Ages, material rewards were indeed far more opulent in China and India (Elvin; Landes; Greif et al. ), potentially driving the religious divergence between Eastern mysticism and Western activism.
Crucially, this economic origin of religious divergence may help explain the great “reversal of economic fortune” between the East and the West (Acemoglu et al.; Greif et al. ).
Precisely because China and India had long been the economic centers of the Middle Ages, they failed to sustain religious activism that later revolutionized traditional economy in Western Europe.
This reversal is especially likely to emerge under a strong religious motivation in search of salvation, which may override and reverse the direction of material incentives.
We therefore uncover salvation religions as a novel mediator for the great economic reversal (Figure 2).
fell behind in modern growth weak constraints on kings China and India backwardness in traditional economy Salvation religions were more likely to be activist.
pioneered modern economic growth strong constraints on kings Western Europe Second, why did the West manage to impose strong constraints on executive power, but not historical China or India?
In general, how is it ever possible to constraint executive power, which can always inflict massive sufferings on any challenger?
To answer these questions, we integrate our model of salvation religions with a political game of domination, persecution, and resistance.
Under intense persecution by a king, a religious figure of Judeo-Christianity may simply conclude that he suffered because he did not do enough in constraining the king (Finer; Hayes ).
Such a belief propels an even stronger resistance against the king’s domination.
This remarkable transformation of suffering into determined action is rooted in the unique meaning of suffering as interpreted by activist religions.
In sharp contrast to this unwavering resistance is the eventual escapism of Chinese religiocultural elites.
To understand this, we extend the model to incorporate the central leitmotif of Chinese culture, the “complementarity” between Confucianism and Taoism.
Because Confucianism is a religion of world adjustment, it cannot provide enough inner support for Chinese elites who have been persecuted by the emperor.
These elites eventually turn to the spiritual comfort of the mystic Taoism, which promises perfect equanimity out of detachment from worldly actions.
Each subsequent persecution from the emperor propels these elites to escape further from their duty to discipline the emperor’s power.
Finally, how could some world-affirming ideologies still be so dominant, such as Confucianism and the modern secularism (pp.14, Troeltsch )?
World affirmation can protect its plausibility through the alternative route of competitions into elite status, promising that “salvation” is possible, but only for the elites.
These status competition can enchant “commoners” because they cannot directly test whether world affirmation as an elite can free one from all troubles.

Our analysis explains the rise of status competition where the influence of salvation religions have declined, such as the Confucian civil service examination in Imperial China and the fierce status competitions in the modern world (Fisher and van Wees; Abdulkadiroğlu et al.; Bai and Jia; Chen et al.; Turchin ).
The paper is related to a few strands of literature.

Our model attempts to examine different religious paths to cope with suffering, which has been identified as a central motivation of religiosity by a large empirical literature (Norenzayan and Hansen; Belloc et al.; Hood Jr et al.; Bentzen; Bentzen ).
Our model jointly micro-founds the three Weberian types of world religions, uncovering many novel interactions between world religions and secular institutions.

Among others, our model generates a new, religion-based explanation for the great “reversal of economic fortune,” complementing existing literature that pays more attention to the role of institutions and social organizations (Acemoglu et al.; Greif and Tabellini; Greif et al.; Acemoglu and Robinson; Fernández-Villaverde et al. ).
By focusing on religious coping of suffering, we also show that religion may revitalize political resistance by endowing a special meaning with suffering in the hands of a tyrant.

This offers a potential solution to the perennial puzzle on how the “soft” power of culture can possibly constrain the “hard” power of violence, contributing to the burgeoning literature that examines foundations of constitutionalism (Fearon; Chaney; Belloc et al.; Akerlof; Bazzi et al.; Becker and Pfaff; Bazzi et al.; Bisin et al. ).

Our analysis of religious coping follows the belief approach to religion (McCleary and Barro; Bénabou and Tirole; Levy and Razin; Augenblick et al.; Nunn and Sierra; Carvalho et al. ), complementing the seminal insights from organizational approach (Iannaccone; Berman; Barros and Garoupa ) and the cultural transmission approach (Bisin et al.; Bisin et al. ) on religious strictness.
To our knowledge, we also offer the first model to analyze the central leitmotif of Chinese culture, the complementarity between Confucianism and Taoism.
This provides a new and religion-based explanation for why the Chinese regime has long been autocratic (Stasavage; Francois et al.; Egorov and Sonin; Jia et al. ).
Our model also reveals the decline of salvation religions as a novel explanation for the rise of status competition in various contexts (Abdulkadiroğlu et al.; Chen et al. ).
The paper is organized as follows.
Section 2 presents the baseline model.
Section 3 shows that salvation religions can reverse “economic fortune.” Section 4 explores a religiocultural foundation of executive constraints.
Section 5 establishes the linkage between world affirmation and status competition.
Section 6 concludes.
Our objective in Section 2 is to provide a parsimonious model that can still micro-found all three Weberian types of world religions.
This baseline model has an infinite horizon, with the time index t = 1, 2,.. At each period t, an individual chooses an action a.
The action a t admits various interpretations, such as labor supply, wealth accumulation, political engagement, etc.
The variable a t in general represents the worldly action. 2 For each period t, the individual may suffer from a disaster at a cost K > 0.
Is it possible to be internally immune from suffering?
More specifically, will a follower be psychologically liberated from such sufferings through “just” action?
There are two competing worldviews.
The religious worldview versus the naturalist worldview Under the religious worldview, action is “meaningful” in terms of explaining and avoiding suffering.

At each period t, the individual suffers with probability    ̄ ̸ = a h. 0 if and only if a t 2 As a defining feature, salvation religions indeed impose strong ethical rules on everyday actions, much more so than the previous “archaic” religions that focus on sacrifice and rituals (Bellah ).
The religion sanctifies an action a as the religious commandment, promising that the individual who chooses a h can gain a perfect internal immunity against suffering.
This perfect internal immunity ensures that an individual does not suffer even when he is inflicted with a disaster.

But if the individual chooses an action different from a h, the individual suffers with probability ̄ “commandment for salvation” (pp.218, Weber ). “Salvation” here is psychological, referring to an “internal immunity against suffering” (pp.218, Weber; pp.66, Weber ).
Such psychological immunity is supported by faith in religious salvation.
Yet because psychological immunity is fundamentally this-worldly, it is related to but not the same as the theological state of religious salvation.
Consider the “extreme” example of the Protestant doctrine of Predestination, where action cannot bring religious salvation at all.
But the psychological confirmation of “certainty of grace” is still achieved primarily through action, central to the famous thesis of Protestant Ethic (Weber; Troeltsch ).
This framework is the fundamental method of both Weber and Tillich.
This framework directs our attention away from the exact theological state of religious salvation to how the quest for psychological immunity influences secular institutions (Whimster ).
Appendix A provides much more details regarding the Weberian definition of salvation religions.
The religious worldview competes with a “naturalist” or mechanical worldview.
Under the naturalist worldview, at each period t, the individual suffers with a probability λ M that is independent of the individual’s action:
’s own action is “meaningless” in terms of explaining or avoiding suffering.

In addition, ̄ ̄? We assume an asymmetric process in belief updating. 3 It is straightforward to incorporate a set of sanctified action: under our setup, there is generically a unique optimum among a closed set of sanctified action.
If the individual suffers, he uses Bayes rule to update his belief on − 1 − 1 λ H + (1 − μ t − 1 ) λ M.
If the individual does not suffer, he maintains his belief as the previous period:
The asymmetric updating reflects a much stronger demand for “making sense” of sufferings than good fortunes.
Under the asymmetric updating, the individual thoroughly uncovers all the implications of a traumatic experience for his worldviews, but he does not do so when he has just experienced good fortune.
The asymmetric updating resonates with the “negativity bias,” a fundamental and highly robust finding in psychology (Baumeister et al.; Rozin and Royzman; Vaish et al. ).

Specifically, human beings display a marked “propensity to attend to, learn from, and use negative information far more than positive information”(Vaish et al. ). 4 Even though the asymmetric updating might be closer to the actual updating process, we also show that a model with a fully Bayesian individual preserves all key insights.
Details are in Appendix F.

Suffering in our model When our paper refers to suffering, it refers to pain that is brought by external disasters, or the term 4.
We are not talking about, for example, the self-inflicted pain by choosing an action a t away the secular optimal action a m ( θ ).
Many salvation religions actually puts a high value on such a self-inflicted pain, which results from denying material impulses (Troeltsch ).
From the last period the individual inherits μ t − 1, his belief on the religious worldview.
The individual chooses his action a t.
The individual may suffer from a disaster.
The individual updates his belief that the religious worldview is correct, obtaining μ t.
The robustness of the setup The baseline model is robust to a forward looking individual (Appendix E) and full Bayesian updating (Appendix F).
Because these extensions are largely technical results, we leave them to appendices.
We first define two basic types of religions, “stable” religions versus “unstable” religions.
A stable religion is maximally resilient against sufferings.
Even when the individual has suffered for an arbitrarily large number of times, the individual will not abandon the religion.
Instead, the individual will establish a full and unwavering belief in its “salvation” promise.
From here on only stable religions are called salvation religions, since only their salvation promises are fully convincing in the long run.
By contrast, an unstable religion will eventually be abandoned by the individual after a sufficiently large number of sufferings. 6 The following proposition characterizes stable religions.
A religion is stable if and only if its commandment a h is sufficiently far away from the secular optimal action a m ( ̄ ̄ ̄ ̄ ̄ ̄ ̄ λ H K, suffering from disobedience as expected by the religion.
v ( θ ) F ′ − C ′ ( a ′ ( − a h; μ t − 1 ) | {z } =0 at a h = 0 The individual moves closer to a h when he feels more guilty of disobeying the commandment of his God (a larger μ t − 1 ).
But such an individual still never exactly performs a h if the distance between a h and a m ( θ ) is sufficiently large.
Here comes the key mechanism of the model.
Because the individual never exactly obeys the religious commandment, he can always make better “sense” of suffering through the religious worldview than the naturalist worldview.
After suffering from a disaster, the individual 6 In principle, there can be “intermediate” religions where the belief μ ( i ) does not converge to the two extremes.
We will see that such “intermediate” religions do not exist in our model.
reasons that he suffers because his God was inflicting a just punishment on a “rebellious” follower.

This religious explanation is more convincing than the naturalist explanation because a just God or cosmos should inflict a punishment with a higher probability on a rebellious follower than a morally indifferent world.
As the individual suffers, he updates his belief of worldviews, obtaining an ever stronger faith in his religion.
In the long run, the individual approaches full faith.
Though such an individual still fails to exactly obey the commandment a h, his long-run action has moved as close as possible to a h.

Remark: the centrality of guilt in religious experience and action In our model, guilt is always experienced by a follower of a salvation religion because the strict commandment generates an endogenous gap between the God’s commandment at a h and human action at ̸ = a h.

This may explain why guilt is such a crucial emotion in the religious experience of great salvation religions (pp.35-66, Nietzsche; pp.242-243, Weber; pp.48-50, Tillich ). 7 Note that inner guilt is also indispensable for a salvation religion to guide human action.
Without inner guilt ( H = 0), a religion become a purely intellectual exercise because even a fully believing follower still choose = a m that only maximizes the secular payoff.
Proposition 1 is illustrated in Figure 3.

When a religion sanctifies an action a h too close to the secular optimal action a m ( θ ), in the long run the individual will exactly obey the religious commandment at a h.
When he suffers again, the individual is bound to lose all his faith with the religion, fully adopting the naturalist worldview.

Such an individual will choose what his human nature dictates because his action is meaningless in deliverance from suffering. 7 For example, Weber states that, for followers of salvation religions, “all action in the civilized world [.] appears to be burdened with the greatest guilt (pp. 242, Weber ).” Figure 3: Religious stability and a Weberian typology of world religions We now highlight how Proposition 1 and Proposition 2 manage to micro-found Max Weber’s typology of world religions through a corollary.

section_name: A micro-founded Weberian typology of world religions
text: Our model therefore fully micro-founds Weber’s typology of world religions in Figure 1: a world-adjusting religion that affirms human nature has too little “tension with the world,” unable to offer a credible path of salvation.

section_name: A micro-founded Weberian typology of world religions

In the baseline model, the two cutoffs that separate the three types of world religions are a ( θ, ̄ ̄ ̄ ̄ λ H K as the religious motivation.
By doing so, the model offers a novel answer to two fundamental questions in comparative religion and comparative development:
Why did Judeo-Christianity become far more activist than salvation religions of China and India, such as Buddhism, Hinduism, and Taoism?
Did salvation religions play a significant role in the great reversal of economic fortune between the East and the West?
This section shows that it was the economic backwardness of Western Europe in the Middle Ages that drives its salvation religions to be far more activist than Chinese or Indian ones.
To demonstrate this, we investigate how economic productivity θ affects the likelihood of different types of salvation religions.
Specifically, assume that the commandment a h is a random variable.
The “initial” probability density function (PDF) of a h is l ( · ) over the support [0, ∞ ), with the cumulative probability function L ( · ).
The “final” PDF of religions for x / ̄ ̄ λ H K )] is:
That is, for an unstable religion with a h ̄ ̄ λ H K ) i, its followers will eventually be disenchanted.
These followers will be matched with a stable religion.
The “probability” of matching with a specific stable religion a h is determined by Note: the figure shows changes when economic productivity increases from θ to ̄
The proposition is visualized by Figure 4.
We obtain Proposition 3 by showing that a higher economic productivity ̄ ̄ ̄ ̄ “salvation” promise by keeping a sufficient distance from worldly impulse.
To maintain such a distance, salvation religions tend to be mystic precisely when worldly action is productive.
In this case, it is a superhuman commandment to ask individuals to renounce the lucrative return from worldly action.

Followers of mystic religions can only partially escape from lucrative action, so they can always attribute their suffering to their hesitation to detach from a world that offers such lucrative and rich benefits. 8 This process of re-enchantment is micro-founded later in Section 4.
This protects the promise of internal immunity through mystic escape.
A similar logic drives salvation religions to be activist precisely when worldly action produces few material benefits.
Productivity under traditional economy and the great religious divergence Proposition 3 may help explain the great religious divergence between the East and the West.
While activism and mysticism were both present in many salvation religions, the economic condition might determine which tendency is more dominant.
Salvation religions were more mystic in China and India because they were the centers of traditional economy in the Middle Ages (Abu-Lughod; Landes; Greif et al. ).
By contrast, there were more activist denominations in Judeo-Christianity because Western Europe had long been on the economic periphery. 9 3.2
Based on the logic of Proposition 3, this section shows that salvation religions might be a central mediator that reversed the “economic fortune” between the East and the West.
Consider any economic outcome Y t that is an increasing function of action a t:

For example, early Islam were activist as “a religion of world-conquering warriors;” but as Islam conquered and controlled the economic centers of the Middle East, Sufi mysticism became prominent (pp.269, Weber ).

   West:â ( x ) 1 − L (ā ( θ, ̄ ̄ λ H K ),ā ]; East:ã ( x ) L ( a ( ̄ θ, ̄ ̄ θ, ̄ ̄ ̄ θ,ã h )) increases with ̄ λ H K. In other words, when individuals care more about religious deliverance from suffering ( ̄ λ H K increases), a reversal of economic fortune is more likely to happen.
Proposition 4 shows that it is religious motivation that drives the reversal of economic fortune.
Proposition 4 and the previous Proposition 3 formally establish the solid arrows in Figure 5.
Compared to the West, the higher productivity in the East increased the material reward for action.
Yet this material motivation must compete with the religious motivation on the opposite direction.
When ̄ λ H K increases, individuals cares more about religious deliverance from suffering.
More painful suffering presses individuals to obey salvation religions on the margin, an obedience that will discredit these religions.
Driven by this more intense search for “salvation,” individuals move more towards mystic escape in the East and more towards activism in the West, which tends to further reverse economic fortune.
To summarize, the reversal of economic fortune is again driven by the central principle that salvation religions tend to command the opposite of material impulse.
Another major payoff from modeling salvation religions is to shed light on a perennial puzzle in constitutionalism: how it is possible to constrain executive power?
More specifically, through the ultimate weapon of political violence, can’t a powerful chief executive always persecute any opponents into submission?
We show that it is the activist religion that can transform the experience of being violently persecuted into even stronger determination to constrain the chief executive.
This remarkable transformation is rooted in the unique capacity of activist religions to endow meaning with suffering that revitalizes action.
We embed our model of religions with a simple political game.
A religio-cultural elite may resist the domination from a king, while the king may persecute the elite.
For each period t = 1, 2,., the timeline is as follows.
The king chooses to dominate at D. Domination succeeds with probability Q ( a t ), with Q ′ < 0, and Q ′′ ′ ( a ) = 0.
With probability ν ( a t ), a crisis hits the king’s throne, which the king values at ( · ).
The king can retain his throne by persecuting the elite at a cost ′ > 0 and κ ′ > 0.
If a king loses his throne, he will be replaced with another king who is ex ante identical.
The elite updates his belief, obtaining μ t.
The king’s problem is simple.
At Stage 4, the king persecutes the elite if and only if R ’s problem at Stage 3 is:

Now we focus our attention on the religio-cultural elite.
Similar as the baseline model in Section 2, the religio-cultural elite considers two worldviews.
Under each worldview, the probability that the elite suffers from political persecution is:
At the start of each period t, the elite believes that his religion is true with probability μ t − 1.
At the end of the period t, the religio-cultural elite updates his belief in the same manner as the baseline model.
The payoff to the elite is very similar to the baseline model payoff:

To ensure that there is a unique optimal resistance, assume D ′′ ( a t ) 0 so that the marginal benefit from resistance in deterring domination decreases with the level of resistance. 10 We can then define the corresponding secular optimal resistance:
There exists a unique cutoff a ( ̄ λ H K ) < a m and a unique cutoffā ( ̄ λ H K ) > a m, such that the religious worldview a h is stable if and only if a h < a ( ̄ λ H K ) or a h >ā ( ̄ λ H K ).
Consider a h >ā ( ̄ ̄ λ H K, persecution on an disobeying elite as expected by the religious worldview, which is useful for Section 4.3.
The king’s stark dilemma under activist religions The second part of Proposition 5 captures the central dilemma of a king who faces an “activist” religio-cultural elite ( a h >ā ( ̄ λ H K )).
If the king does not persecute such an elite, the king risks losing his throne immediately.
But if the king does persecute the elite, the elite will resist a tyrannical king even more enthusiastically in the future, forcing the king to reduce future domination.
The elite is so resolute because of his religious belief, that the elite suffers from political persecution because he has not done enough in resisting the king.
Persecution therefore fortifies the elite’s faith in the activist religion, propelling the elite to forever stand firm in constraining the king.

This “titanic and inflexible will” (Fukuyama ) of Western religious figures in their struggle against kings is extremely well documented by historians. 10 Recall that D ′ < 0, so D ′′ 0 implies a decreasing marginal benefit from resistance, a reasonable assumption.
Appendix C elaborates on the two paradigmatic cases of religious figures in Jewish kingdoms and Western Europe of the Middle Ages.

section_name: Confucianism, Taoism, and the great political divergence
text: In Imperial China, the dominant religio-cultural elites are the Mandarins, who are the scholar-officials in the Chinese imperial bureaucracy.

section_name: Confucianism, Taoism, and the great political divergence
text: Mandarins embody the central leitmotif of Chinese culture, the “complementarity” between the world-adjusting Confucianism and the mystic Taoism ( Rudao Hubu, see Li ).

section_name: Confucianism, Taoism, and the great political divergence
text: To model this, the belief system μ t − 1 is a vector of beliefs on two religious worldviews:

section_name: Confucianism, Taoism, and the great political divergence
text: μ t − 1 = ( μ c t − 1, μ d t − 1 ), where μ c t − 1 − 1 (0, 1) is the belief on Taoism, and (1 − μ c t − 1 − μ d t − 1 ) is the belief on the naturalist worldview.

section_name: Confucianism, Taoism, and the great political divergence
text: Under the three worldviews, the probability of suffering from political persecution in the game of Section 4. is:

section_name: Confucianism, Taoism, and the great political divergence
text:          ̄ λ C 1 { a t ̸ = a c } under Confucianism; ̄ λ D 1 { a t ̸ = a d } under Taoism; λ M under the naturalist worldview.

section_name: Confucianism, Taoism, and the great political divergence
text: Assume that ̄ λ C > ̄

section_name: Confucianism, Taoism, and the great political divergence
text: 2. a d < a ( ̄ λ C K ), a m < a c <ā ( ̄ λ C K ) where a ( ̄ λ C K ) andā ( ̄ λ C K ) are as defined in Proposition 5 by setting ̄ λ H = ̄ λ C.

section_name: Confucianism, Taoism, and the great political divergence
text: The first part states that the secular payoff is higher under the Confucian commandment a c than under the Taoist commandment a d, reflecting Confucianism’s closer alignment with secular motives compared to Taoism.

section_name: Confucianism, Taoism, and the great political divergence
text: The second part further states that Confucian commandment a c is between a m and the cutoffā ( ̄ λ C K ) > a m, which is specified in Proposition 5.

section_name: Confucianism, Taoism, and the great political divergence
text: Therefore, as a world-adjusting religion, there is a small distance between the Confucian commandment a c and the secular optimal action a m.

section_name: Confucianism, Taoism, and the great political divergence
text: In the Chinese tradition, Confucianism is also considered “active,” therefore a c > a m.

section_name: Confucianism, Taoism, and the great political divergence
text: Only a comparison with Judeo-Christianity reveals that Confucianism is not active enough.

section_name: Confucianism, Taoism, and the great political divergence
text: By contrast, the mystic Taoism promises that permanent equanimity flows from “nonaction” ( wu wei ), attaining freedom through detachment from the profane world.

section_name: Confucianism, Taoism, and the great political divergence
text: This is captured by the assumption that a d < a ( ̄ λ C K ), which implies that a d < a ( ̄ λ D K ).

section_name: Confucianism, Taoism, and the great political divergence
text: We also impose a regularity assumption on the guilt function to simplify our analysis.

section_name: Confucianism, Taoism, and the great political divergence
text: The assumption says that if a religious worldview is sufficiently unlikely, the individual does not feel internal guilt of acting against its commandment.

section_name: Confucianism, Taoism, and the great political divergence
text: We are now ready to state the main proposition that formalizes the dynamic “complementarity” between Confucianism and Taoism, along with the serious political consequences.

section_name: Confucianism, Taoism, and the great political divergence
text: Suppose that a Mandarin starts with a sufficiently strong belief in Confucianism and a sufficiently weak belief in Taoism: ’s resistance increases over each persecution.

section_name: Confucianism, Taoism, and the great political divergence
text: Therefore, the emperor’s domination decreases over each persecution. 2.

section_name: Confucianism, Taoism, and the great political divergence
text: After the ( I + 1) -th persecution, the Mandarin’s belief in Confucianism is μ c t − 1 = 0. 3.

section_name: Confucianism, Taoism, and the great political divergence
text: If the Mandarin has been persecuted more than I + 2 times, the Mandarin’s resistance decreases over each new persecution.

section_name: Confucianism, Taoism, and the great political divergence
text: So the emperor’s domination increases over each new persecution.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: The baseline model begs a further question.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: How could Confucianism be so dominant in Imperial China, or for any other world-adjusting religions?

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: This section investigate an alternative route to protect the “salvation” promise.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: A world-adjusting religion can establish a competitive contest that certifies an “elite” status, promising that world adjustment achieves “salvation,” but only for certified elites.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: Salvation through such an elite status is credible among “commoners” who are unable to directly falsify the promise.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: But these status contests are unlikely to emerge under a salvation religion because their strict commandments are already sufficient to protect their credibility.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: There is indeed a close connection between world affirmation and status contests:

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: • In Imperial China, the entire society long revolved around the civil service examination that conferred the elite status of Mandarins (Bai and Jia; Chen et al. ).

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: • The competition over socioeconomic status is fierce in the “modern world,” whose dominant culture affirms humanity itself “as the source of truth and moral conduct” (pp.14, Troeltsch; Turchin ).

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: In our model, these status contests sustain the credibility that world affirmation can free one from all troubles.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: Equally important, we will show that only a culture of world affirmation can induce status contests at such extravagance.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: To formalize these ideas, we extend the baseline model by introducing two social classes, elites and commoners.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: At the start of each period t, there is a measure

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: Π( e i t; e − i t ), where e − i t = Z j [0, 1] \{ i } e j t dj is the average level of effort of other commoners.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: Assume that Π( e i t; e − i t ) increases with e i t and decreases with e − i t, reflecting the tournament nature of such a contest.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: The naturalist or mechanical worldview is the same as the baseline model.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: The (modified) religious worldview is:

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: λ H That is, one is free from troubles if one obeys the commandment a h as an elite.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: Elites are privileged here because they have an opportunity to attain “salvation.” The payoff to an individual i is as follows.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: v ( θ ) F ( a i t ) − C ( a i t )+ H ( a i t − a h; μ t − 1 ) − [ μ i t − 1 ̄ λ H · 1 { a i t ̸ = a h or i is not an elite } | {z } the only difference in payoff +(1 − μ i t − 1 ) λ M ] K.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: The only difference from the payoff in the baseline model is highlighted (except the superscript i in the action a i t and the belief μ i t − 1 ).

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: To reiterate, the only difference is that “salvation” is always unattainable to an individual as a commoner.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: We simplify the analysis by looking at a situation where all commoners are identical and experience common shocks.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: We can then only track the belief and the strategies of a representative commoner.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: So we ignore the superscript i for the representative commoner, and his belief is simply μ t − 1.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: The timeline of the game for each period t is as follows.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: All individuals inherit their beliefs from the last period.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: A qβ measure of elites retire and exit the game.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: Commoners chooses efforts e i t for all i − 1 from existing commoners.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: All individuals choose their actions.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: All individuals, including the elites and commoners, may suffer.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: All individuals update their beliefs.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: Specifically, commoners obtain μ t.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: We impose standard regularity conditions for this simple game of status contests.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: The second-order condition for e i t requires a decreasing marginal return to one’s own effort ( ′ > 0, Γ ′′ > 0).

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: To ensure the uniqueness of the equilibrium, we also assume the following condition.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: Assumption 3. − i t < 0.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: This assumption guarantees a regular best response function: a commoner reduces his effort if other commoners increase their effort.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: Without this assumption, there is a strategic complementarity among the efforts of commoners, a complementarity that could generate multiple equilibria in efforts.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: There is a unique symmetric equilibrium in the commoners’ efforts in the status contest, denoted as e ̄ ̄ λ H K )] as defined in Proposition 2.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: (a) Every time the representative commoner suffers, the more believing commoner exerts a (weakly) higher effort in the contest.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: World affirmation supports status competition Under a world adjusting religion, commoners believe that elites have attained the commandment that free them from all troubles.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: Therefore, there is a striking contrast between the elites in permanent bliss and commoners in permanent danger of suffering, as believed by commoners.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: Commoners can sustain this belief because they have never experienced the life of elites.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: This belief then drives the fierce competition into the elite status.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: The competition into the elite status is especially extravagant when the religion strongly affirms the “human nature” (a very small distance between a h and a m ( θ )), so that the life of an elite is believed to be especially blissful: the elite attains “salvation” simply by fulfilling his humanly desires.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: Salvation religions discourages status competition Status competition is not necessary for salvation religions, which can fully protect their credibility by their superhuman commandments.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: So salvation religions do not need to set up status competition in the first place. 11 What if a salvation religion still does so?

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: Part 2 of Proposition 7 investigates this situation.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: Commoners never contest the elite status because they realize that elites, even though endowed with the opportunity to attain “salvation,” still cannot do so because no humans can fulfil the superhuman commandment.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: Suffering is driven by the constitutive imperfection in “human nature” that transcends social classes (pp.330, Weber ).

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: The theoretical analysis is summarized in Figure 7, showing an alignment between status competition and world adjusting religions.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: Appendix D discusses in detail the implications of the theoretical analysis, focusing on the civil service examination in Imperial China, as well as the prominence of status competition in the modern world.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: Appendix D also shows that our results are robust under modified setups.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: Commoners may compete elite status for both the opportunity of “salvation” and an extra material benefit B > 0.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: Commoners may also feel no guilt from disobedience before they succeed in the status competition.

section_name: Religious tendencies and competition for status
text: In either case, there is still a strong alignment between status competition and world adjustment.

Our analysis of religions is motivated by a central principle that only the unattainable can enchant, the principle that drives individuals to move away and transcend their “human nature.” This self-transcendence has been identified as essential to religion. [R]eligion is the capacity of human organism to transcend its biological nature through the construction of objective, morally binding, all embracing universes of meaning (pp.176, Berger ).

In our model, the religious worldview can achieve objectivity as full belief and morally binding force as a strong guidance of human action, but only when it demands the transcendence 11 This can be easily formalized by introducing a “designer” for any religion a h [ a,ā ] at t = 0, before the game starts at t = 1.
A designer wants to maximize a function V ( x ) that increases with x, the number of followers in the long run.
The designer pays a cost D if he sets up a status competition.
Suppose that V (1) > D, so it pays to set up the status competition if it is necessary to induce full faith among commoners.
It is obvious that a designer introduces a status competition if and only if the religion is world-adjusting.
beyond human will power to protect its plausibility (Proposition 1).
This view of salvation religions has been proven to be analytically fruitful.
Among others, the paradoxical nature of self-transcendence offers a novel explanation for the great reversal of economic fortune between the East and the West.

Salvation religions can reverse economic fortune because self-transcendence demands world renunciation in a productive economy and world mastery in a backward economy (Proposition 3 and Proposition 4).
We also show that salvation religions as self-transcendence may explain how it is ever possible to constrain political power (Section 4).

Violence as the ultimate weapon of political power can be neutralized by an unwavering conviction in the value of self-sacrifice to resist tyranny, a conviction that is paradoxically driven by a selfish human nature.

By showing how a species of “selfish genes” (Dawkins ) can fervently believe in the exalted value of self-sacrifice, we uncover a new explanation for the possibility of constitutional order (Joas and Knöbl ).

The principle of unattainability can also bridge the analysis of salvation religions and “quasi-religions,” especially the quest for socioeconomic status that are extremely difficult to attain (Tillich; Tillich ).
Section 5 shows that the principle shed light on why these “quasi-religions” are especially dominant in a culture of world affirmation, where the influence of salvation religions has declined.
We therefore follow the Tillichian program (Tillich ) to analyze religions and “quasi-religions” under a unified principle, which also helps reveal their fundamental differences.
Transcendence of selfish nature may generate many other unintended consequences.
Mechanisms that could preserve peace among self-interested individuals, such as the “commercial peace” (Hirschman ), began to crack.
Novel forms of conflicts could emerge, conflicts that were unthinkable among purely self-interested individuals.
Further investigation is warranted for the many paradoxical relations between actions and ideologies that are sustained by the “unattainability” condition.
